docs.title: My Digital Garden
docs.text:
Welcome! I am Abby, an undergraduate student in the Turing Class (Computer Science and Technology) at Zhejiang University.

This website is a live record of my intellectual growth and technical journey. It serves as a centralized repository where I document my ongoing explorations into the frontiers of research.

docs.title: 📚 What I am doing here
docs.text:
• Mathematical Foundations: Building a solid base in Analysis, Algebra, and Probability.
  • Programming & Systems: Mastering languages (C/C++, Python) and hardware architecture (Verilog).
  • Research Exploration: Investigating Computer Vision and Embodied AI and so on.
  • Toolkit & Workflows: Curating efficient software practices and site maintenance guides.

docs.title: 🚀 Continuous Learning
docs.text:
I believe in "Learning in Public." This site is under constant construction as I acquire new skills and refine my understanding. Every update here represents a step forward in my academic and professional path.

Stay curious. Keep building.

Last updated: 2026 | Built with MkDocs

docs.title: Hello, I'm Abby! ☁️

docs.title: Programming & Systems
docs.text:
——building from the metal up 🦀

Programming is more than just writing logic; it is about understanding how data flows through silicon. My focus lies in Low-Level Systems, Hardware Architecture, and High-Performance Computing, avoiding the "black box" approach of modern high-level frameworks.

docs.title: 📝 Current Tech Stack
docs.text:
• C/C++: The "Mother Tongue" for systems and performance-critical 3D Vision.
  • Verilog: Designing logic at the gate level (Hardware Architecture).
  • Python: Essential for scripting and Deep Learning research.
  • Assembly (x86/RISC-V): Understanding the ISA layer.

docs.title: 1. No Black Boxes
docs.text: I prefer manual memory management over Garbage Collection. Understanding malloc and free is non-negotiable for anyone serious about system performance.

docs.title: 2. Efficiency Over "Business Logic"
docs.text: I find more satisfaction in optimizing a Spatial Partitioning Tree or a Gaussian Splatting Renderer than in building repetitive CRUD applications.

docs.title: 3. The Hardware-Software Interface
docs.text: Bridging the gap between high-level algorithms and the actual hardware (CPU/GPU) is where the real engineering happens.

docs.title: ⚙️ C/C++ Development
docs.text: Focus: Manual memory management, STL internals, and high-performance rendering.

docs.title: 🐍 Python Scripting
docs.text: Focus: PyTorch for AI research and automation scripts for workflows.

docs.title: 🔌 Hardware Design (Verilog)
docs.text: Focus: Finite State Machines (FSM), RTL design, and FPGA prototyping.

docs.title: 🌐 Toolkit & Environment
docs.text:
(How I build things...)

  • Editor: VS Code / Neovim (for the terminal soul).
  • Compiler: gcc / clang / iverilog.
  • Build System: CMake & Makefile.
  • Version Control: Git (GitHub-based workflow).

"Low-level programming is good for the soul." — John Carmack

docs.title: C/C++ Development: Mastery of the Machine
docs.text:
——where the abstraction ends and the hardware begins 🔌

C and C++ are the languages of High-Performance Computing and Computer Vision. My approach is centered on understanding memory layout, pointer arithmetic, and the zero-overhead principle.

docs.title: 📝 To-Do List
docs.text:
• C Fundamentals: Manual memory management (malloc/free) and pointer logic.
  • Modern C++ (17/20): RAII, Smart Pointers, and Templates for type-safe generic programming.
  • Data Structures: Implementing Linked Lists, Trees, and Hash Maps from scratch in C.
  • Performance Profiling: Using gdb and valgrind to hunt memory leaks and bottlenecks.

docs.title: 1. Manual over Automatic
docs.text: I prefer direct control over memory. No <ctype.h> or high-level abstractions if a simple character comparison or a bitwise operation can do the job faster.

docs.title: 2. The Power of Pointers
docs.text: Understanding void*, pointer decay, and function pointers is essential for building flexible system-level software without the bloat of OOP.

docs.title: 3. Zero-Cost Abstractions
docs.text: C++ is used when templates or classes provide a cleaner architecture without sacrificing a single CPU cycle of performance.

docs.title: 🌟 3D Vision Kernels
docs.text:
Writing efficient math routines for point cloud processing and transformation matrices.

  • Status: Prototyping

docs.title: 📁 Custom Data Structures
docs.text:
A collection of "No-Standard-Library" implementations for embedded-style constraints.

  • Status: Continuous

docs.title: 📖 The Classics
docs.text:
• The C Programming Language (K&R): The bible of C. Concise and perfect.
  • Effective C++ (Scott Meyers): Essential for writing robust, modern C++ code.

docs.title: 🛠️ Toolchain
docs.text:
• Compiler: gcc / g++ (Strictly using -Wall -Wextra -Werror).
  • Debugger: GDB (The terminal is my best friend).
  • Build: Makefiles for small projects, CMake for everything else.

docs.title: 🧩 Code Snippets
docs.text:
C

/* Manual memory allocation & Pointer arithmetic */
int *arr = (int*)malloc(10 * sizeof(int));
for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++) {
    *(arr + i) = i * i;
}
free(arr);

"C makes it easy to shoot yourself in the foot; C++ makes it harder, but when you do, it blows your whole leg off." — Bjarne Stroustrup

docs.title: Python Scripting: The Bridge to Intelligence
docs.text:
——high-level agility for low-level minds 🕸️

While C/C++ handles the "how," Python handles the "what." In my workflow, Python serves as the essential glue for Deep Learning, 3D Computer Vision research, and automating the mundane parts of site maintenance.

docs.title: 📝 To-Do List
docs.text:
• PyTorch Foundations: Tensors, Autograd, and building Neural Networks.
  • Data Science Stack: Mastering NumPy, Matplotlib, and OpenCV.
  • Automation: Scripting MkDocs deployments and file organization.
  • Blender API: Scripting 3D scene generation for synthetic data.

docs.title: 1. Prototyping Speed
docs.text: Python is the sandbox where ideas for Gaussian Splatting or Spatial Intelligence are tested before they are optimized in C++.

docs.title: 2. Deep Learning Ecosystem
docs.text: From torch to diffusers, Python is the gateway to the state-of-the-art. I use it to implement papers and evaluate pre-trained models.

docs.title: 3. Clean & Functional
docs.text: I aim for PEP 8 compliance and favor functional approaches (list comprehensions, map/filter) to keep scripts concise and readable.

docs.title: 🏗️ Core Libraries
docs.text: Library Purpose Status NumPy Linear Algebra & Array ops 🌕 Mastered PyTorch Neural Rendering / CV Research 🌗 Learning OpenCV Image Processing & Calibration 🌗 Learning Requests Web Scraping & API Interaction 🌕 Mastered

docs.title: 📂 Workflow Snippets
docs.text:
Python

import torch

# Simple Linear Layer in PyTorch
class SimpleNet(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc = torch.nn.Linear(3, 1) # 3D coord to 1D intensity

    def forward(self, x):
        return torch.sigmoid(self.fc(x))

docs.title: 📖 Literature & Docs
docs.text:
• Python Crash Course (Eric Matthes): Great for quick syntax refreshing.
  • Dive into Deep Learning (d2l.ai): An interactive guide to the math and code of AI.

docs.title: 🛠️ Environment
docs.text:
• Package Manager: conda / mamba for isolated research environments.
  • Interactive: Jupyter Notebooks for quick experiments and visualization.

"Python is executable pseudocode." — Bruce Eckel

docs.title: Hardware Design: Thinking in Parallel
docs.text:
——where code becomes silicon ⚡

Verilog is not a programming language; it is a Hardware Description Language (HDL). Moving from C to Verilog requires a paradigm shift: from sequential execution to massive parallelism and precise timing.

docs.title: 📝 To-Do List (Turing Class)
docs.text:
• Combinational Logic: Mastering gates, muxes, and ALUs.
  • Sequential Logic: Flip-flops, registers, and the sanctity of the always @(posedge clk) block.
  • Finite State Machines (FSM): Designing robust Moore and Mealy machines for control logic.
  • CPU Architecture: Building a basic RISC-V pipeline from scratch.

docs.title: 1. Everything Happens at Once
docs.text: In Verilog, all assign statements and separate always blocks execute concurrently. Understanding "race conditions" at the gate level is the ultimate test of logic.

docs.title: 2. Blocking vs. Non-Blocking
docs.text:
• Blocking (=): For combinational logic—immediate evaluation.
  • Non-Blocking (<=): For sequential logic—scheduling updates for the next clock edge. Mixing these up is the fastest way to break a design.

docs.title: 3. Resource Constraints
docs.text: Unlike software, hardware is finite. Every module consumes Look-Up Tables (LUTs) and Flip-Flops. Optimization here means reducing area and increasing maximum frequency (\(f_{max}\)).

docs.title: 📂 Simple Counter (The "Hello World" of HDL)
docs.text:
Verilog

module counter (
    input clk,
    input rst_n,
    output reg [3:0] count
);
    always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n) begin
        if (!rst_n)
            count <= 4'b0000;
        else
            count <= count + 1'b1;
    end
endmodule

docs.title: 💻 Local Environment
docs.text:
• Simulator: iverilog (Icarus Verilog) for lightweight testing.
  • Waveform Viewer: GTKWave — Essential for debugging signal timing.
  • Synthesis: Vivado (for Xilinx FPGAs) or open-source flows like Yosys.

docs.title: 📖 References
docs.text:
• Digital Design and Computer Architecture (Harris & Harris) - The bridge from gates to MIPS/RISC-V.
  • HDLBits: For practicing Verilog syntax through problem-solving.

"Software can be patched; hardware is forever."

docs.title: Mathematics
docs.text: ——something hard but useful :crying_cat_face:​

docs.title: 📝 To-Do List
docs.text:
• Mathematical Analysis II (this term)
  • Linear Algebra (with LADR，this term)
  • Probability & Statistics (next term)
  • Ordinary Differential Equations (self-explore)
  • Discrete Mathematics（this term）

docs.title: 🌐 External Resources
docs.text: (These are just recources that I'm using...)

docs.title: 🎓 Courseware & Open Courses
docs.text:
• MIT OpenCourseWare (18.01, 18.02, 18.06) Recommended by my teachers (but somehow it doesn't fit my Mathematical Analysis Courses very well)
  • Stanford Engineering Everywhere (SEE) - Gemini recommend this
  • ZJU CKC materials from seniors - VERY USEFULL if you're a CKC student!!!

docs.title: 🎥 Visual Learning
docs.text:
• 3Blue1Brown (Essence of Linear Algebra/Calculus) - Essential for geometric intuition.
  • Bright Side of Mathematics - Rigorous and clear video lectures.

docs.title: 🛠️ Interactive Tools
docs.text:
• Wolfram|Alpha - Computational intelligence engine.
  • Desmos Graphing Calculator - Best for visualizing functions.
  • Overleaf (LaTeX) - For professional academic writing.
\[ f(x) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{f^{(n)}(a)}{n!} (x-a)^n \] \[ \hat{f}(\xi) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) e^{-2\pi i x \xi} dx \]

“Mathematics is the language in which God has written the universe.”

docs.title: Mathematical Analysis: The Calculus of Rigor
docs.text:
——where intuition meets \(\epsilon - \delta\) 🔍

Mathematical Analysis is the formalization of "closeness." For a CS student interested in 3D Vision and Embodied AI, this isn't just about solving integrals—it's about understanding the continuity and optimization of the physical world.

docs.title: 📝 To-Do List (Spring 2026)
docs.text:
• Multivariable Calculus: Partial derivatives, Gradients, and the Jacobian matrix.
  • Sequences & Series: Understanding convergence and Taylor approximations.
  • Optimization Foundations: Lagrange multipliers and Hessian matrices for surface analysis.
  • Measure Theory: Long-term goal for a deeper understanding of probability.

docs.title: The Linear Approximation
docs.text: A derivative isn't just a slope; it's the best linear approximation of a function at a point. In 3DGS or Neural Rendering, we constantly linearize complex systems to make them computable.

docs.title: From 1D to \(\mathbb{R}^n\)
docs.text:
The jump from single-variable to multivariable analysis is where the magic happens.

  • The Gradient (\( abla f\)): Points in the direction of steepest ascent.
  • The Divergence (\( abla \cdot F\)): Measuring the "source" or "sink" of a vector field.
  • The Curl (\( abla imes F\)): Essential for understanding rotations in 3D space.

docs.title: 🛠️ Essential Formulae
docs.text: \[L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a)\] \[\nabla f(a) = \left( \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1}(a), \dots, \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_n}(a) \right)\]

docs.title: 📖 Literature
docs.text:
• Principles of Mathematical Analysis (Rudin): The "Gold Standard" for rigor. Hard, but undeniably useful.
  • Calculus on Manifolds (Spivak): A modern approach to multivariable analysis using differential forms.

docs.title: 🎥 Visual Resources
docs.text:
• 3Blue1Brown - Essence of Calculus: For when the symbols get too abstract and I need to see the area under the curve.
  • ZJU Course Materials: Keeping my pace synchronized with the Turing Class curriculum.

"The purpose of analysis is to find the hidden simplicity in complex systems."

docs.title: Linear Algebra: The Geometry of Data
docs.text:
"The purely formal handle on algebra is a bit like a dry bone; the geometric intuition is the meat." — Adapted from 3Blue1Brown

Linear Algebra is the engine behind Computer Vision and Spatial Intelligence. Following the "Axler Way" (LADR), I focus on linear maps and operators rather than just pushing numbers around in matrices.

docs.title: 🎯 Current Focus (LADR Pathway)
docs.text:
• Vector Spaces & Subspaces: Understanding the "canvas" where linear transformations live.
  • Linear Maps: Treating matrices as representations of functions between vector spaces.
  • Eigenvalues & Eigenvectors: Finding the invariant directions of a transformation.
  • Inner Product Spaces: The foundation of geometry—orthogonality, projections, and the Adjoint operator.

docs.title: 🔍 3D Vision Applications
docs.text:
• Change of Basis: Crucial for transforming coordinates between Camera Space, World Space, and Screen Space.
  • Singular Value Decomposition (SVD): The "Swiss Army Knife" for solving Overdetermined Systems and Essential Matrix estimation.
  • Projective Geometry: Understanding how 3D points map to 2D planes via Homographies.

docs.title: 📝 Key Insights
docs.text:
• Determinants are Volume: A determinant isn't just a formula; it's the scaling factor of a transformation's volume.
  • Spectral Theorem: Why symmetric matrices (like Covariance matrices) are so well-behaved and useful for PCA.

docs.title: 🛠️ Working Sandbox
docs.text:
• Implement basic Matrix Decomposition in pure C (for the "Low-level" soul).
  • Visualize Span and Linear Independence using Manim/Python.
  • Master the Gram-Schmidt Process for orthonormalization.

docs.title: Research Exploration: The 3D Vision Frontier
docs.text:
——reconstructing the world from pixels to voxels 🌌

This page documents my journey into Computer Vision and Embodied AI. My goal is to move beyond 2D image processing and explore how machines can perceive, represent, and interact with the 3D physical world.

docs.title: 📝 To-Do List (Freshman Year & Beyond)
docs.text:
• Foundations: Mastering Image Formation, Epipolar Geometry, and Camera Calibration.
  • Deep Learning: Understanding CNNs, Transformers, and MLP-based architectures.
  • Neural Rendering: Diving deep into 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) and NeRF.
  • Lab Integration: Preparing for research in 3D reconstruction and spatial intelligence.

docs.title: 1. Neural Graphics & 3DGS
docs.text: Traditional meshes are rigid. I am fascinated by point-based and volume-based representations that allow for real-time, high-fidelity rendering. 3D Gaussian Splatting represents a breakthrough in balancing speed and quality.

docs.title: 2. Spatial Intelligence
docs.text: How does an agent understand "where" it is? I am exploring the intersection of SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) and deep features to build more robust environmental priors for robots.

docs.title: 3. Embodied AI
docs.text: The ultimate test of vision is action. I am interested in how 3D representations can be used for robot manipulation and navigation in unstructured, real-world environments.

docs.title: Phase 1: The Geometry (Current)
docs.text:
• Concepts: Projective Geometry, Essential/Fundamental Matrices, Bundle Adjustment.
  • Tools: OpenCV, Eigen, C++.

docs.title: Phase 2: The Neural Era
docs.text:
• Concepts: Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF), Differentiable Rendering, Point-based Neural Rendering.
  • Tools: PyTorch, CUDA, Tiny-CUDA-NN.

docs.title: Phase 3: The Interaction
docs.text:
• Concepts: Reinforcement Learning for Vision, Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models.
  • Tools: Isaac Gym, Habitat-Sim.

docs.title: 📄 Milestone Papers
docs.text:
• Gaussian Splatting for Real-Time Radiance Field Rendering (Kerbl et al., 2023)
  • NeRF: Representing Scenes as Neural Radiance Fields for View Synthesis (Mildenhall et al., 2020)
  • End-to-End Object Detection with Transformers (DETR) (Carion et al., 2020)

docs.title: 🏫 ZJU Labs of Interest
docs.text:
• ZJU-3DV: Leading research in 3D reconstruction and neural rendering.
  • Embodied AI Groups: Exploring the bridge between perception and robotics.

"The best way to predict the 3D future is to render it."

docs.title: Site Maintenance: Building the Garden
docs.text:
——automating the flow from thought to web 🏗️

This site, Abby's Wonderland, is powered by MkDocs and the Material theme. It is designed to be a lightweight, version-controlled repository of my academic and technical growth.

docs.title: 📝 Maintenance Checklist
docs.text:
• Local Preview: Running mkdocs serve to verify \(\LaTeX\) rendering.
  • Automated Deploy: Setting up GitHub Actions for seamless gh-pages updates.
  • Asset Optimization: Compressing 3D meshes and high-res images for faster loading.
  • Structure Audit: Ensuring the mkdocs.yml navigation stays clean as the garden grows.

docs.title: 1. The Local Sandbox
docs.text:
I write in Markdown using VS Code or Obsidian. To see changes in real-time:

Bash

# Spin up the local dev server
mkdocs serve

Access via localhost:8000.

docs.title: 2. The Build Process
docs.text:
When a module (like a new CV research note) is complete, I build the static site:

Bash

mkdocs build

This converts all Markdown files, \(\LaTeX\) math blocks, and code snippets into optimized HTML/CSS.

docs.title: 3. Shipping to the Web
docs.text:
I use GitHub Pages for hosting. The deployment is a single command:

Bash

mkdocs gh-deploy

docs.title: Material for MkDocs
docs.text: Chosen for its high-performance search, mobile responsiveness, and "Admonitions" (those useful callout blocks).

docs.title: MathJax / KaTeX
docs.text:
Essential for rendering the heavy lifting of Mathematical Analysis and 3DCV equations.

YAML

# In mkdocs.yml
extra_javascript:
  - javascripts/mathjax.js
  - https://polyfill.io/v3/polyfill.min.js?features=es6
  - https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/mathjax@3/es5/tex-mml-chtml.js

docs.title: 📂 Directory Structure
docs.text:
Plaintext

.
├── docs/
│   ├── math/          # Analysis, Linear Algebra
│   ├── coding/        # C++, Verilog, Python
│   ├── research/      # 3DCV & Embodied AI
│   └── index.md       # The "Digital Garden" landing page
├── mkdocs.yml         # Site configuration & Navigation
└── requirements.txt   # Python dependencies

docs.title: 💡 Future Enhancements
docs.text:
• Interactive 3D: Integrating three.js or Gradio components to showcase 3D reconstructions directly on the page.
  • Dark Mode Toggle: For those late-night coding sessions in the Turing Class lab.
  • Search Optimization: Better indexing for deep technical terms.

"A digital garden is never finished; it only evolves."

docs.title: Software & Tools: The Digital Workshop
docs.text:
——crafting an environment for maximum friction-less focus 🛠️

A researcher is only as good as their tools. I prioritize tools that are lightweight, customizable, and keyboard-centric, allowing me to bridge the gap between abstract thought and technical execution.

docs.title: 📝 Current Workflow To-Do
docs.text:
• Dotfiles Management: Version controlling my system configurations via Git.
  • Neovim Mastery: Transitioning from VS Code for terminal-based C/C++ development.
  • Docker Containers: Isolation for reproducible Deep Learning environments.
  • Markdown/LaTeX: Streamlining the "Note-to-Publication" pipeline.

docs.title: 🐧 Linux (WSL2 / Ubuntu)
docs.text:
The native habitat for Computer Vision and Systems Programming.

  • Shell: zsh with Oh My Zsh for a clean, informative prompt.
  • Terminal: Windows Terminal (customized) or Alacritty.
  • Multiplexer: tmux for persistent sessions during long model training.

docs.title: 🧩 VS Code
docs.text:
My primary IDE for complex C++ projects and Python research.

  • Essential Extensions: C/C++ (IntelliSense), Pylance, LaTeX Workshop, and GitHub Copilot.
  • Vim Mode: Enabled. Navigating code without a mouse is non-negotiable.

docs.title: 🖋️ Obsidian / MkDocs
docs.text:
My "Second Brain."

  • Obsidian: For fleeting notes and daily logs.
  • MkDocs: The engine powering Abby's Wonderland (this site), converting technical notes into a structured digital garden.

docs.title: 🔬 Research & Data Stack
docs.text: Category Tool Why? VC Git / GitHub The standard for collaboration and versioning. Math WolframAlpha Quick verification of integrals and matrix props. Viz MeshLab Inspecting 3D point clouds and Gaussian Splats. Docs Overleaf For collaborative \(\LaTeX\) writing.

docs.title: 📦 Development
docs.text:
• gcc / gdb: The fundamental toolchain.
  • CMake: The only way to manage C++ builds sanely.
  • Conda: Keeping Python environments from becoming a dependency hell.

docs.title: 🎨 Design
docs.text:
• Desmos: For rapid mathematical visualization.
  • Figma: Occasionally used for poster layouts and UI prototyping.

"First, build the tools. Then, build the world."
